data.title: TSX edges higher on BoC, Fed rate cut optimism
data.description:
By Twesha Dikshit

Canada's main stock index rose on Thursday, with tech shares offsetting declines in gold and mining stocks, a day after the Bank of Canada and the U.S. Federal Reserve cut rates by a quarter-point and left the door open for further reduction.

Toronto's S&P/TSX composite index gained 0.3% to 29,420.03 points by 10:00 ET (1400 GMT) in choppy early trading.

The BoC reduced its key policy rate to a three-year low of 2.5% in a unanimous decision, adding that it would be ready for further cuts if the economy needed them.

The Fed also expressed a dovish sentiment, projecting two more cuts for the year.

"Markets continue to ride the positive momentum after central banks showed that they are willing to respond to some economic softness and also cooling in the labor market," said Angelo Kourkafas, senior investment strategist at Edward Jones.

"The TSX has been riding the gold wave and we have seen the biggest rally in gold since the 1970s. It will be important to see if there's some pullback in gold, what impact it would have on the material sector."

Gold miners (.SPTTGD) and material stocks led the sectoral declines, tracking a drop in metal prices. Gold prices pulled back from a recent rally.

Miner Endeavour Silver Corp dropped 4.5%, while Seabridge Gold , Orla Mining and G Mining were down between 2% and 3%.

Conversely, technology stocks rose 1.3% after weakness in the previous session.

Electronic equipment company Celestica added 1.1%, while heavyweights Shopify and Blackberry were up 2% and 3.3%, respectively.

Meanwhile, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney arrived in Mexico City on Thursday in an attempt to improve recently strained ties with Mexico and seek a common front ahead of trade talks with the U.S.

The crucial U.S.-Mexico-Canada free trade deal will be reviewed next year.

data.title: South African rand flat after central bank keeps key rate unchanged
data.description:
The South African rand was largely flat after the central bank kept interest rates unchanged as it cautiously monitored the effect of previous cuts.

At 1402 GMT, the rand traded at 17.41 against the dollar , near Wednesday's closing level.

South Africa's central bank kept its key interest rate (ZAREPO=ECI) unchanged at 7%, in line with economists polled by Reuters.

"Since September last year, we have reduced rates by 125 basis points, and we want to see how this is affecting the economy, how expectations evolve, and how inflation risks are resolved," Governor Lesetja Kganyago said on Thursday.

"The rand has been surprisingly weak post the SARB rates decision," said Shaun Murison, senior analyst at Rand Swiss, adding that the SARB's upward revision to its growth forecast for this year from 0.9% to 1.2% would be welcomed.

Some analysts had thought that the central bank would cut rates after headline inflation in Africa's most industrialised economy unexpectedly slowed in August due to softer fuel and food prices.

On the Johannesburg Stock Exchange, the Top-40 index was also little moved.

South Africa's benchmark 2035 government bond (ZAR2035=) was flat as the yield rose half a basis point to 9.175%.

data.title: Darden Restaurants Price Target Maintained With a $235.00/Share by TD Cowen
Darden Defends Tipping Model — Market Talk
data.description:
1011 ET - McDonald's recently took a swipe at the fairness of the tipped wage model, leading it to withdraw from the National Restaurant Association trade group. Full-service restaurant owner Darden says that policy groups should reflect all of its constituents' interests, and defended the tipped wage. "As a full service operator, our business model continues to be the best choice for our guests and our team members," the company says during a 1Q call. Darden says it will be able to adapt if changes to the tipped wage occur. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

1010 ET - Affordability concerns, which have led to stagnant home sales, are now leading to slowing homebuilding activity, say D.A. Davidson analysts Kurt Yinger and Aditya Madan. Total housing starts in August were down 6% year-over-year and single-family starts were down 12%. Home affordability challenges are hurting single-family building activity, "while consumer confidence, trade policy/ broader economic uncertainty, and elevated levels of completed unsold home inventory are other factors that serve as cold water being added to single-family embers," the analysts say. Even though mortgage rates have fallen, the economy's labor market has weakened, dampening expectations for a turnaround in home sales. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0951 ET - National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said on CNBC that he wasn't aware of anyone in the government negotiating with Nvidia to encourage it to invest in Intel. The U.S. announced it was taking a 10% stake in Intel last month and Hassett says Intel wasn't meeting the marks it needed to in order to receive funds from the CHIPS Act. "Instead of just throwing money away into a business that may or may not succeed, the U.S. government is helping the business succeed and making it so that the taxpayer benefits if they're successful at that," Hassett says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0936 ET - Restaurants are closely watching how GLP-1 weight loss drugs impact their sales, and Darden says its testing smaller portion sizes at its Olive Garden brand with an eye to these consumers. Olive Garden has put seven smaller entrees on the menus of about 40% of its restaurants, with the meals also costing less than other options. Executives say on an F1Q call that the options contribute less to sales than regular-size entrees, but believe they will boost customer traffic. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

0933 ET - Higher beef costs are squeezing restaurant chains, including Darden. The LongHorn Steakhouse owner reported higher food and beverage costs in F1Q, with beef growing more than expected. The company says it has less of its beef buying locked down in contracts than usual, but executives believe costs will eventually come down and want to remain flexible. Darden sinks 8% in early trading. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

0928 ET - Oil futures are flitting between small gains and losses as the market waits to see what additional sanctions the EU will impose against Russia. The possibility of disruptions in Russian oil supply has supported prices the past week, while concerns about higher OPEC+ output keeps a lid on gains. This week's large U.S. crude inventory draw and the Fed's interest rate cut "have helped to keep the losses in check," ING strategists Ewa Manthey and Warren Patterson say in a note. WTI is flat at $64.05 a barrel and Brent edges up 0.1% to $68.01. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0921 ET - Novo Nordisk has clear leadership in the field of orally-taken obesity treatments following trial data for its Wegovy weight-loss pill and a competing tablet from Eli Lilly, UBS analyst Matthew Weston writes. Lilly had already released headline data for its Orforglipron pill, delivering up to 12.4% weight-loss at the highest dose, below the 16.6% weight-loss in a trial of Novo Nordisk's pill, Weston adds. Further data at this week's EASD medical conference in Austria showed Orforglipron weight-loss plateaued relatively early and even saw weight regain at the 12 milligram dose, he adds. "Orforglipron still presents a threat on being a more scalable product, and therefore potentially discounted price." It also doesn't require 30 minutes fasting when taking it, so could be seen as more convenient, he says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Getting a $5 billion investment from Nvidia is a game-changer for Intel that now positions it firmly in the AI market, say Wedbush analysts. "Along with the recent US Government investment for 10% this has been a golden few weeks for Intel after years of pain and frustration for investors," the analysts say. A partnership with Nvidia, which has the most advanced chips in the world, positions Intel to meaningfully contribute to AI development in the U.S. "Today's announcement further strengthens the US lead in the AI Arms Race against China as Intel now goes from a laggard to a catalyst," the analysts say. Intel surges 29% premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0907 ET - There is no clear sign that a buyer for Puma has been identified but it is possible talks have been held in recent weeks to gauge investor interest, analysts from JP Morgan write in a note. Shares in the German sportswear company jumped as much as 17% Wednesday, after a report from German Manager Magazin suggested Reebok owner Authentic Brands and CVC Capital Partners were mulling a purchase of the Pinault family's 29% stake. They add that an imminent acquisition is unlikely due to the challenges of finding a buyer and upon any agreement being reached at an attractive premium. Puma need to focus on repositioning its brand and clearing out its distribution network, the analysts say. Shares are down 2.4% at 22.72 euros. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0859 ET - Fresh U.S. data suggest the Fed may not need to hurry monetary easing, triggering a bonds selloff and sending Treasury yields and the dollar higher. Weekly jobless claims fall to 231,000 from an upwardly revised 264,000. Economists surveyed by WSJ expected 240,000. September's Philadelphia Fed activity survey surprises on the upside. The data comes after the Fed cut interest rates for the first time this year, indicating more easing to come. Odds of an October cut are priced at 88%, CME data show. The 10-year Treasury yield is at 4.085% and the two-year at 3.551%. The WSJ Dollar Index rises 0.2%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0809 ET - Intel is getting another high-profile boost. A month after Softbank said it was investing $2 billion and the U.S. government said it was taking a 10% stake in the troubled chipmaker, Intel is now getting a $5 billion investment from Nvidia. The company has fallen behind competitors, who have emerged to lead the lucrative business of supplying AI chips. Unlike the U.S. government's stake, Nvidia's investment could provide a boost in actual chip development capability that Intel investors have been looking for by giving the company a powerful partner in the chip industry. Intel surges 29% premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0726 ET - Shares of European semiconductor companies are on the rise after Nvidia said it would inject $5 billion into Intel as part of a collaboration to develop custom data-center and personal-computing products. Shares of Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment ASML Holding and smaller rival ASM International are up nearly 8%. Shares of BE Semiconductor Industries, the Dutch supplier of semiconductor assembly equipment, rise 5.4%. German chip maker Infineon Technologies logs a 4.1% gain. Shares of STMicroelectronics, which supplies to clients like Elon Musk's Tesla and Apple, rise 5.2%. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: 89bio Stock Soars 86%. Why Roche Is Buying the Biopharma Company for $3.5 Billion. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Shares of 89bio surged Thursday after Roche announced it was buying the small-cap biopharmaceutical company for $3.5 billion.

As part of the deal, 89bio shareholders will receive up to $20.50 a share in cash, which includes $14.50 a share at closing and a non-tradeable contingent value right of up to $6 a share.

89bio is a late-stage biopharma company valued at $1.2 billion. Its primary asset is pegozafermin, a drug candidate that mimics the chemical structure of a hormone secreted by the liver.

Pegozafermin has yet to secure approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, and is undergoing clinical trials exploring its efficacy at treating conditions commonly linked to obesity, such as a type of fatty liver disease and hypertriglyceridemia.

Upon closing of the deal, 89bio will become part of Roche's pharmaceuticals division, the drugmaker said.

"We are excited about this agreement and to further develop this promising therapy," Boris Zaïtra, the head of Roche Corporate Business Development, said in a statement. By adding pegozafermin to Roche's portfolio, "we are aiming to transform the standard of care and positively impact patients' lives," he added.

Shares of 89bio soared 86% to $14.99 on Thursday, putting the stock on pace for its largest one-day percentage increase on record, according to Dow Jones Market Data. U.S.-listed shares of Roche were down slightly, while the benchmark S&P 500 index was up 0.6%.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

data.title: Workers at key UK gas plant Shell Bacton vote for strike in pay dispute
data.description:
Workers for industrial group Altrad at the Shell Bacton Gas terminal on Britain's North Sea coast have voted in favour of strike action in a bid to get Altrad to raise pay, the GMB union said on Thursday.

Bacton Gas Plant handles up to a third of Britain's gas supply fed by North Sea gas fields as well as importing and exporting gas from continental Europe, according to Shell's website.

"It is in the best interests of Altrad management to reflect on this result, listen to our members, and come back to the table urgently," said GMB Regional Organiser Paul Block.

"Failure to resolve this dispute will result in strike action, which would bring work on this site to a standstill."

Shell did not immediately reply to a request for comment.

data.title: Inside Microsoft's Plans for the 'Most Advanced AI Data Center in the World' — WSJ
data.description:
By Isabelle Bousquette

Microsoft said it is in the final stages of construction on the $3.3 billion project in Wisconsin it is calling the most advanced AI data center in the world.

The project will come online in early 2026 and initially will be used to train OpenAI models, said Microsoft Vice Chair and President Brad Smith. In the future it could also include other tenants, including Microsoft itself, he said.

Speaking on Thursday from Wisconsin, Smith also unveiled an additional $4 billion commitment from Microsoft for a second data center in the area, of similar size and scope.

Smith said a combination of the number of Nvidia GPUs, the power of those GPUs and the networking and systems that connect all the compute power will make the project so cutting edge.

Smith added, "You put all of this together and it makes it possible to train more advanced AI models than have ever been trained before."

The initial data center includes three buildings with a combined 1.2 million square feet across 315 acres. Inside, the facility hosts hundreds of thousands of Nvidia GB200 GPUs, the company said. At the rack level, these GPUs communicate at terabytes per second.

The data center is also set up as two-story construction, allowing for closer networking and reducing latency, Microsoft said. Construction on the project required 120 miles of medium-voltage underground cable and 72.6 miles of mechanical piping.

"The frontier where the most powerful models are being built, we continue to see this kind of scale being essential," Smith said.

The newly announced $4 billion data center will be its third AI datacenter in the so-called Fairwater family of advanced AI datacenters. The first is the original Wisconsin build, and the second is located in Georgia.

Smith said Microsoft secured the land from Taiwanese iPhone assembler Foxconn, who once heralded a $10 billion investment in the area that largely failed to materialize. "We were able to buy the land. And we benefited from that early stage infrastructure and preparation," he said.

The data center's cooling system is another aspect that makes it cutting edge, Smith said.

"One of the big questions that actually is emerging in Wisconsin right now is whether companies are building data centers close to Lake Michigan in order to use massive amounts of water from the lake. The answer is no," he said.

More than 90% of the facility will be served by a closed-loop system that recirculates the water and chemicals it is filled with during the construction phase and doesn't need to be refilled.

The remaining portion will use outside air cooling, and only switch to water when necessary on hot days. Microsoft said that as a result, the total annual water usage of a single data center on the campus would require roughly the equivalent of what a typical 18-hole golf course uses a week in peak summer months or what a restaurant uses in a year.

Smith said he is also sensitive to local resident concerns about data center energy usage driving up energy prices for local residents. Microsoft negotiated higher rates for itself with the local utility provider, WE Energies, to bear that cost, he said.

Write to Isabelle Bousquette at isabelle.bousquette@wsj.com

data.title: Darden Raises Sales Outlook, Warns Rising Costs Will Squeeze Profit — Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Darden Restaurants raised its full-year sales outlook but cautioned that higher food and labor costs, coupled with the company's push to keep prices low, will hurt earnings in the near term.

The owner of Olive Garden and LongHorn Steakhouse restaurants is absorbing higher costs to avoid raising prices.

"The largest investment we made over the past several years is pricing below total inflation," Chief Financial Officer Raj Vennam said during a call with analysts Thursday.

Currently, though, costs are rising quicker than anticipated. Vennam said the company saw higher-than-expected beef costs toward the end of the recent quarter, as well as a further increase in labor costs.

These headwinds have pressured earnings, and Darden's shares. The stock was recently down 8.8% to $190.45. Despite the decline, the shares are up nearly 20% over the past year.

Darden logged a 10% jump in sales during its fiscal first quarter, boosted by higher same-restaurant sales across its portfolio. Budget-conscious consumers are still willing to eat out, Chief Executive Rick Cardenas said, though he added value and flexibility are becoming increasingly important.

During the recent quarter, Olive Garden tested smaller-portion entrees at lower prices to appeal to value-seeking diners, as well as to consumers using weight-loss drugs. Cardenas said the move was well received and is likely to boost traffic, despite resulting in smaller average checks.

And at Ruth's Chris Steak House, diners are seeking more price certainty, driving strong demand for a new fixed-price three-course menu, Cardenas said. He also pointed to continued growth in delivery, which is drawing younger, convenience-minded customers.

For the three months ended Aug. 24, the Orlando, Fla., company reported a profit of $257.8 million, or $2.19 a share, up from $207.2 million, or $1.74, a year earlier. Adjusted earnings were $1.97 a share, slightly below the $2 a share that analysts polled by FactSet expected.

Quarterly sales rose 10% to $3.04 billion, in line with Wall Street models.

Same-restaurant sales, those from stores and digital channels in operation for at least 12 months, climbed 4.7% across the company's portfolio. The increase was led by a 5.9% gain at Olive Garden and a 5.5% gain at LongHorn Steakhouse, slightly offset by fine-dining, which ebbed 0.2%.

Looking ahead, Darden raised its full-year sales growth target to between 7.5% and 8.5%, up from a prior range of 7% to 8%. Same-restaurant sales are now expected to rise 2.5% to 3.5%, while the company affirmed adjusted earnings guidance for $10.50 a share to $10.70 a share.

Vennam said that the company expects its lowest year-over-year earnings per share growth this year in the current quarter due to rising beef costs and its price investments.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Pound and gilts fall after shrugging off BoE balancing act
data.description:
Key points:
  • Pound, gilts decline after holding steady post BoE decision
  • Rate and QT decision widely expected
  • Mismatch seen between investor, market rate expectations

By Jaspreet Kalra, Naomi Rovnick and Canan Sevgili

The pound slipped and gilt yields rose on Thursday, reversing course after having offered a muted reaction after the Bank of England kept rates unchanged and slowed the pace of its government bond holding reductions.

Analysts said the shift appeared to stem more from stronger U.S. employment data that boosted the dollar broadly and sent bond yields higher than from the BoE's policy steps.

Sterling was down 0.6% at $1.3541, while the yield on the benchmark 10-year gilt rose 5 basis points to 4.67%.

"I don't see any sort of clear catalyst for the move, besides the fact that it seems to have gathered momentum since U.S. traders entered the fray," said Michael Brown, senior research strategist at Pepperstone.

Earlier, BoE policymakers voted 7-2 to keep rates unchanged at 4% while slowing the annual pace at which the central bank sells gilts it purchased between 2009 and 2011 to 70 billion pounds from the current 100 billion pounds, in line with economist forecasts.

UK inflation is almost twice the central bank's 2% target, and there are growing signs of weakness in the labour market. Yet money markets are only fully pricing in another rate cut by March next year, something some investors believe may be overly pessimistic.

"There could be good news on the horizon that allows a few more cuts than the market expects. Commodity prices are not going up and that may weigh on inflation," said Christopher Mahon, multi-asset manager at Columbia Threadneedle.

MOST STAGFLATIONARY DEVELOPED ECONOMY

"The UK is now the most stagflationary economy in the developed world. A brutal mix of high inflation, weak growth, and rising unemployment," said Lloyd Harris, head of fixed income, Premier Miton Investors.

"The next big moment for the BoE and for all sterling markets is the Autumn Budget," Harris said.

Finance Minister Rachel Reeves will present her budget on November 26. She is under pressure to stick to her own rules on borrowing to keep Britain's finances on track, as reflected in some of the large swings in yields on long-term UK bonds this year.

The central bank said it would skew sales away from long-dated gilts to minimise the impact on turbulent bond markets. Britain's 30-year borrowing costs had climbed to their highest level since 1998 earlier this month but have since eased.

"The Bank of England's decision to slow its pace of bond sales was fairly consensus, but the decision to skew those sales towards shorter maturities should provide some relief to the long-end," said Matthew Landon, global market strategist at J.P. Morgan Private Bank in London.

The 30-year gilt yield (GB30YT=RR) was last up 6 bps at 5.493%, tracking a similar sized move in its U.S. counterpart. Germany's 30-year bond yield was up 6 bps as well at 3.30%.

The BoE is alone among major central banks in conducting outright sales of the government bonds it bought to boost the economy in the years after the 2008 global financial crisis, rather than just letting them mature.

British stocks held on to their post-BoE gains, with the benchmark FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 stock index last up 0.3%, while the more domestic-focused mid-cap stock index was up nearly 0.4%.

"There’s always going to be some sensitivity around the future inflation profile. And given the background of where the UK has been, I expect the Bank of England to remain cautious," Matt Hudson, UK portfolio manager at River Global, said.

data.title: Nucor Stock Falls After Earnings. Why Its Guidance Was Far Off Rival Steel Dynamics. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Nucor stock fell after giving weaker-than-expected third-quarter guidance.

Shares of the American steel maker dropped 4.4% to $136.50 in early trading Thursday, while S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were up 0.8% and 0.6%, respectively.

The move came after Nucor said on Wednesday evening that third-quarter earnings per share (EPS) should fall between $2.05 and $2.15. Wall Street was looking for $2.46, according to FactSet.

Guidance was a disappointment. Nucor reported EPS of $2.60 in the second quarter of 2025 and $1.05 in the third quarter of 2024.

Nucor noted "sequentially lower volumes and narrower metal spreads in the [steel] mills segment," wrote Citi analyst Alexander Hacking in a Wednesday report. Spreads refer to the difference between steel prices and the prices Nucor pays for raw materials such as steel scrap.

Hacking rates shares Buy and has a $150 target price for the stock. The outlook "contrasts directly with Steel Dynamics' recent guidance for stronger shipments and expanded spread," he added.

Steel Dynamics on Tuesday said third-quarter EPS should fall between $2.60 and $2.64 a share, up from $2.01 in the second quarter and $2.05 the year prior.

Wall Street was expecting EPS of closer to $2.70 a share, but earnings are improving quarter to quarter, and guidance was good enough to send Steel Dynamics stock up 6.1% on Tuesday.

"We would attribute this to Steel Dynamics' idiosyncratic issues at Sinton in the second quarter and a more aggressive commercial strategy," added Hacking. The Sinton mill is Steel Dynamics' relatively new facility in Texas.

Coming into Thursday trading, Steel Dynamics' stock was up about 20% so far this year. Nucor shares were up about 22%.

Higher domestic steel prices, partly due to import tariffs, have boosted benchmark steel prices. Hot rolled coil is selling for about $800 per ton, up roughly $100 year over year. Steel prices are still well below a recent 2021 peak of more than $1,800 per ton.

Steel prices have a powerful effect on steel-maker earnings. Nucor earned $23.16 in 2021. Wall Street expects about $8.20 a share in 2025.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Darden Restaurants Price Target Maintained With a $215.00/Share by Stephens & Co.
Atlassian to acquire developer productivity platform DX for $1 billion
data.description:
Atlassian said on Thursday it has agreed to acquire developer intelligence platform DX for about $1 billion in cash and restricted stock, aiming to improve customer experience by providing insights into AI investments.

Enterprises have ramped up funding in AI capabilities as they compete to leverage the technology for innovation, operational efficiency, and competitive advantage.

In a move underscoring this trend, Atlassian earlier this month announced a $610 million deal to acquire The Browser Company, the maker of AI-powered Dia and Arc browsers.

Companies use DX to collect and analyze data on engineering workflows, measure productivity, track the adoption and impact of AI tools.

Most of DX's enterprise customers such as Pfizer , Pinterest and Xero also use Atlassian to plan and track their work, CEO Mike Cannon Brookes said in a blog.

"Today's announcement is about helping our 300,000+ customers understand if they're making the right investments to win in the AI era," Brookes said.

Atlassian said the deal, expected to close in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, does not change its previously issued fiscal 2027 adjusted operating margin target.
